title: Palliative and End of Life Care
authors: Ezekiel J. Emanuel, John W. Urwin

items.subtopic: Palliative and End-of-Life Care | CAUSES OF DEATH
items.title: Understanding the Epidemiology of Death in the U.S.
items.content: The causes of death have shifted significantly over the past century, with a notable decrease in cardiovascular-related deaths and an increase in cancer deaths. This shift impacts healthcare spending and emphasizes the need for palliative care across various chronic conditions.
keyPoints: In 2019, before COVID-19, 2,854,838 individuals died in the U.S., with 74% of these occurring in those aged ≥65 years.
Heart disease caused ~8% of all deaths in 1900 and decreased to 23.1% by 2021; cancer increased from <4% in 1900 to 21.0% in 2019.
In the U.S., about 90% of health care spending is on patients with chronic illnesses, with ~$450 billion spent on those in their last year of life in 2022.
70% of deaths in upper-middle- and upper-income countries are preceded by a disease or condition.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · Palliative and End-of-Life Care | CAUSES OF DEATH

items.subtopic: Palliative and End-of-Life Care | CAUSES OF DEATH
items.title: The Role of Palliative Care in Chronic Illness Management
items.content: Palliative care is crucial for managing symptoms and improving quality of life in patients with chronic illnesses, including those with predictable terminal phases like cancer, heart failure, COPD, and dementia.
keyPoints: Palliative care benefits all chronically ill patients, regardless of prognosis.
Conditions such as heart failure, COPD, liver failure, and dementia have recognizable terminal phases necessitating systematic end-of-life care.
Coordination between caregivers, doctors, and patients for advance care planning enhances palliative care effectiveness.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · Palliative and End-of-Life Care | CAUSES OF DEATH

items.title: Shift from Hospital to Home Deaths
items.content: There has been a significant shift over the past few decades in the United States and other countries, with more patients preferring to die at home rather than in hospitals. This trend is particularly evident among cancer patients.
keyPoints: In Belgium and Canada, over half of all cancer patients still die in the hospital.
In the U.S., 70% of American cancer patients died in hospitals in the early 1980s; this number has decreased to about 25% today.
From 2000 to 2015, deaths due to cancer in acute care hospitals dropped from 30.1% to 19.8% among Medicare beneficiaries.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · SITE OF DEATH

items.title: Increase in ICU Admissions Near End of Life
items.content: Despite the decline in hospital deaths, there has been an increase in ICU admissions and hospitalizations during the last months of life for cancer patients.
keyPoints: >40% of U.S. cancer patients are admitted to the ICU in their last 6 months of life.
>25% of cancer patients are hospitalized in the last 30 days before death.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · SITE OF DEATH

items.title: Racial Disparities in End-of-Life Care
items.content: Significant racial disparities exist in end-of-life care, with minorities less likely to receive hospice services and more likely to undergo aggressive treatments.
keyPoints: African Americans and Asians/Pacific Islanders are less likely to die at home or in hospice for head and neck cancers.
Racial and ethnic minority patients are 22% less likely than white patients to have hospice care before death after a pancreatectomy for pancreatic cancer.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · SITE OF DEATH

items.subtopic: HOSPICE AND THE PALLIATIVE CARE FRAMEWORK
items.title: Early Integration of Palliative Care for Symptom Management and Transition Planning
items.content: Integrating palliative care early in the course of a serious illness, rather than waiting until the final months, allows for better symptom management and smoother transition to hospice care. This approach provides patients and families with time to adjust to the progression of the disease.
keyPoints: Palliative care should begin many months before death to focus on symptom relief.
Transitioning to hospice typically occurs in the patient's last few months.
Patients often receive palliative interventions long before being formally diagnosed as terminally ill or likely to die within 6 months.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · HOSPICE AND THE PALLIATIVE CARE FRAMEWORK

items.subtopic: HOSPICE AND THE PALLIATIVE CARE FRAMEWORK
items.title: Interdisciplinary Team Approach in Palliative and Hospice Care
items.content: An interdisciplinary team approach is central to palliative and hospice care, addressing not only physical symptoms but also providing spiritual, psychological support for patients, and assistance for family caregivers during illness and bereavement.
keyPoints: Palliative and hospice care involves an interdisciplinary team.
Focus areas include pain and symptom management, spiritual and psychological care for the patient.
Support is extended to family caregivers during both the illness period and bereavement.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · HOSPICE AND THE PALLIATIVE CARE FRAMEWORK

items.title: Comprehensive Assessment Tools in Palliative Care
items.content: A comprehensive assessment in palliative care should include standardized tools to evaluate physical, psychological, and existential symptoms. These assessments help identify sources of suffering and their impact on quality of life.
keyPoints: Use validated instruments like the revised Edmonton Symptom Assessment Scale, Condensed Memorial Symptom Assessment Scale (MSAS), MD Anderson Brief Symptom Inventory, Rotterdam Symptom Checklist, Symptom Distress Scale, Patient-Reported Outcomes Measurement Information System, and ISAAC tool.
Screen for mental health issues with tools such as PHQ-9 for depression and GAD-7 for anxiety.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · ASSESSMENT AND CARE PLANNING (part 1)

items.title: Avoidance of Invasive Tests in End-of-Life Care
items.content: In end-of-life care, invasive tests should be minimized due to their limited benefit-to-burden ratio. Physical examinations that are uncomfortable and unlikely to change management should also be avoided.
keyPoints: Minimize use of invasive tests; assess benefit-to-burden ratio carefully.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · ASSESSMENT AND CARE PLANNING (part 1)

items.title: Effective Communication in Palliative Care
items.content: Empathic and effective communication is crucial during emotionally charged moments, such as delivering bad news or discussing prognosis. Proper preparation can lead to better patient outcomes and family bereavement.
keyPoints: Develop a standardized approach for sharing important information.
27% of families in ICU desired better and more private space for discussions.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · ASSESSMENT AND CARE PLANNING (part 1)

items.title: SPIKES Protocol for Delivering Bad News
items.content: The SPIKES protocol is a structured six-step process designed to facilitate the communication of bad news in clinical settings. It emphasizes creating an appropriate environment, understanding patient perceptions, gauging their readiness for information, delivering knowledge empathetically, allowing emotional responses, and summarizing next steps.
keyPoints: SPIKES consists of six steps: set up a suitable environment, understand patient/family perception, obtain invitation to receive new information, provide needed knowledge, allow for emotional responses, summarize and share next steps.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · ASSESSMENT AND CARE PLANNING (part 2)

items.title: Importance of Continuous Goal Assessment in Palliative Care
items.content: Continuous goal assessment is crucial in palliative care to align treatment with patient and family goals, which can range from curing a disease to finding peace of mind. This involves a seven-step protocol that includes understanding medical information, exploring hopes and realistic goals, sharing options, empathetic response, planning, follow-through, and periodic review.
keyPoints: Goals for care include curing diseases, prolonging life, relieving symptoms, adapting to disability, finding peace of mind, and ensuring positive memories for loved ones.
Seven-step protocol: complete information understanding, explore hopes/goals, share options, empathetic response, make a plan, follow through, periodic review.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · ASSESSMENT AND CARE PLANNING (part 2)

items.title: Advance Care Planning Statistics and Challenges
items.content: Despite widespread endorsement of advance care planning, many adults lack completed directives. A study found that while 42% needed to make end-of-life decisions, 70% lacked capacity, with only about one-third having advance directives. Older adults are more likely to complete these documents than younger ones.
keyPoints: 42% of adults required to make treatment decisions in final days lacked decision-making capacity.
Approximately one-third of those lacking capacity had no advance planning directives.
80% endorse advance care planning, but fewer have completed documents (38.2% chronically ill, 45.6% older adults).
45% claim to have a living will; estimates suggest 26-67% lack completed advance care documents.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · ASSESSMENT AND CARE PLANNING (part 2)

items.title: Effective Advance Care Planning Steps
items.content: Advance care planning involves six key steps: introducing the topic, structuring a discussion, reviewing plans with the patient and family, documenting the plans, updating them periodically, and implementing advance directives. Barriers include difficulty in raising the topic and structuring discussions.
keyPoints: Six key steps for effective advance care planning
Barriers: issues in raising the topic and structuring discussions
Routine introduction recommended for all patients
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · ASSESSMENT AND CARE PLANNING (part 3)

items.title: Types of Advance Care Planning Documents
items.content: Advance care planning documents include living wills and health care proxies. Living wills are advisory, detailing patient preferences for care scenarios. Health care proxies designate an individual to make decisions. Combined directives can specify precedence between patient preferences and proxy choices.
keyPoints: Living wills: instructional directives with specific or general statements
Health care proxies: designated individuals to make decisions
Combined directives: specify precedence in case of conflict
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · ASSESSMENT AND CARE PLANNING (part 3)

items.title: Legal Aspects of Advance Care Planning
items.content: As of 2024, 48 states and the District of Columbia have living will legislation. Massachusetts and Michigan lack such laws; Indiana has a life-prolonging procedures declaration. State requirements vary for witnessing and notarization. In 26 states, living wills are invalid if pregnant. All but Alaska allow proxies to terminate life-sustaining treatments.
keyPoints: 48 states plus D.C. have living will legislation
Massachusetts and Michigan lack living will laws
In 26 states, living wills are not valid during pregnancy
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · ASSESSMENT AND CARE PLANNING (part 3)

items.title: Constitutional Right to Refuse Life-Sustaining Interventions
items.content: Patients have a constitutional right to refuse or terminate medical interventions, including life-sustaining ones. This right extends to mentally incompetent patients who can provide 'clear and convincing evidence' of their preferences through advance care directives.
keyPoints: Advance care directives are constitutionally protected.
States must honor clear advance care directives, even if not on an official form.
Many states have laws to honor out-of-state directives.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · ASSESSMENT AND CARE PLANNING (part 4)

items.title: Reimbursement for Advance Care Planning Discussions
items.content: Since January 1, 2016, Medicare reimburses advance care planning discussions under CPT codes 99497 and 99498. These sessions must be voluntary and include an explanation of advance care planning but do not require a completed document.
keyPoints: Reimbursement for advance care planning began in 2016.
CPT codes 99497 and 99498 are used for billing discussions.
Only 1.6% of Medicare Advantage patients had billed discussions in 2016.
15% of practices billed for any advanced care planning visits from 2016 to 2018.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · ASSESSMENT AND CARE PLANNING (part 4)

items.title: Pain Management in Palliative Care
items.content: While pain management is crucial in palliative care, making it the fifth vital sign has not improved practices. Emphasis now includes nonpharmacologic treatments and identifying psychosocial risk factors for addiction.
keyPoints: Pain was once considered the fifth vital sign but hasn't led to better management.
Nonpharmacologic pain treatment is recommended alongside pharmacologic methods.
Psychosocial risk factors for addiction should be identified.
Opioids are a cornerstone of end-of-life pain management, despite the opioid crisis.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PHYSICAL SYMPTOMS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.title: Pain Prevalence and Types in Terminally Ill Patients
items.content: The prevalence of pain varies by illness, with cancer having the highest incidence. Pain is categorized into nociceptive (somatic or visceral) and neuropathic types.
keyPoints: Pain prevalence: Cancer (~85%), CHF (~75%), HIV/AIDS (~98%), COPD (~45%), chronic kidney disease (~40%), dementia (~40%).
Nociceptive pain includes somatic (localized, aching) and visceral (deep, colicky).
Neuropathic pain is described as burning or shock-like.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PHYSICAL SYMPTOMS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.title: Assessment and Intervention Strategies for Pain in Palliative Care
items.content: Pain assessment should be systematic, considering type, periodicity, location, intensity, modifying factors, treatment effects, functional impact, and patient impact. Interventions must be individualized, with opioids as a key component.
keyPoints: Assessment includes type, periodicity, location, intensity, modifying factors, treatment effects, functional impact, and patient impact.
Validated pain assessment measures include VAS, DVPRS, BPI, NRS-11, Neuropathic Pain Scale, DN4 Questionnaire.
Interventions should be tailored to preempt chronic pain and relieve breakthrough pain.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PHYSICAL SYMPTOMS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHYSICAL SYMPTOMS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 2) | NOCICE PAIN
items.title: Initial Management of Nociceptive Pain: NSAIDs and Acetaminophen
items.content: Nociceptive pain, characterized by sensations such as cramping, throbbing, aching, sharp, prickling, stabbing, deep and constant, dull and gnawing, can be managed initially with nonopioid analgesics. NSAIDs are preferred for their anti-inflammatory properties, but they have a ceiling effect and potential risks like bleeding and renal impairment. Acetaminophen is an alternative, especially in patients at risk of GI complications.
keyPoints: NSAIDs inhibit peripheral prostaglandins and reduce inflammation with central nervous system effects.
Ibuprofen dosage: 400 mg every 6 h; max 8 tablets per day (2400 mg/d qid).
Acetaminophen dosage: 500 mg, 2 tablets every 4–6 hours.
NSAIDs have a ceiling effect and can cause bleeding and renal impairment.
Ibuprofen is preferred unless there's a history of severe GI or other bleeding.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PHYSICAL SYMPTOMS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 2) | NOCICE PAIN | NEUROPATHIC PAIN

items.subtopic: PHYSICAL SYMPTOMS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 2) | NEUROPATHIC PAIN
items.title: Characteristics and Management of Neurogenic Pain
items.content: Neurogenic pain, described as burning, electrical, or shock-like, arises from conditions such as poststroke pain, tumor invasion of the brachial plexus, or herpetic neuralgia. It requires a different management approach compared to nociceptive pain, often involving opioids in combination with other treatments.
keyPoints: Neurogenic pain is characterized by burning, electrical, shock-like sensations.
Common causes include poststroke pain, tumor invasion of the brachial plexus, and herpetic neuralgia.
Management typically involves NSAIDs or acetaminophen with opioids.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PHYSICAL SYMPTOMS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 2) | NOCICE PAIN | NEUROPATHIC PAIN

items.title: Opioid Initiation and Management for Pain
items.content: For patients with insufficient pain relief from nonopioids, opioids should be introduced. The goal is to prevent pain by administering opioids on a regular schedule before the effect of the previous dose wears off. Short-acting opioids are used initially to determine dosage needs, followed by extended-release preparations once stable pain control is achieved.
keyPoints: Opioids work primarily through $\mu$ opioid receptors in the CNS.
Start with short-acting opioids for initial 24–48 hours to establish dosing requirements.
Switch to extended-release preparations after achieving pain relief with short-acting ones.
Regular, around-the-clock administration is preferred over as-needed use.
Rescue medication should be provided at 20% of the baseline dose for breakthrough pain.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · Treatment (part 1)

items.title: Opioid Dose Adjustment and Switching
items.content: If a patient's pain remains uncontrolled, adjust opioid doses based on rescue medication usage or increase by 50% for moderate pain and 100% for severe pain. When switching opioids due to inadequate relief or side effects, start with 50–75% of the equianalgesic dose of the new opioid.
keyPoints: Adjust daily opioid doses if breakthrough pain occurs before the next scheduled dose.
Cross-tolerance among opioids is incomplete; different opioids may have varying efficacy and side effects.
Begin a new opioid at 50–75% of the equianalgesic dose when switching.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · Treatment (part 1)

items.title: Managing Opioid Side Effects, Particularly Constipation
items.content: Constipation is a common side effect of opioid therapy and should be preemptively managed with fiber supplements, osmotic laxatives, and stimulant laxatives. For refractory cases, consider opioid antagonists or other therapies.
keyPoints: Preemptive treatment for constipation includes psyllium, polyethylene glycol, and senna.
Opioid side effects like constipation should be anticipated and treated to prevent noncompliance.
For refractory constipation, consider opioid antagonists or alternative therapies such as lubiprostone.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · Treatment (part 1)

items.title: Methylnaltrexone for Opioid-Induced Constipation
items.content: Methylnaltrexone is used to treat refractory opioid-induced constipation by blocking peripheral opioid receptors without affecting central analgesia. It typically causes laxation within 24 hours of administration.
keyPoints: Blocks peripheral, not central, opioid receptors
Laxation occurs within 24 hours in placebo-controlled trials
Nausea and vomiting occur in about a third of patients but usually develop tolerance within a week
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · Treatment (part 2)

items.title: Managing Opioid Tolerance and Dependence in Palliative Care
items.content: In palliative care, opioid tolerance is often due to disease progression rather than true addiction. Physical dependence should not be confused with addiction, and opioids should not be withheld for fear of addiction.
keyPoints: Tolerance is usually due to disease progression
Physical dependence vs. addiction: symptoms from withdrawal indicate dependence
Patients rarely become addicted when opioids are used as prescribed
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · Treatment (part 2)

items.title: Adjuvant Analgesics for Neuropathic Pain Management
items.content: Gabapentin is the first-line treatment for neuropathic pain, started at 100–300 mg bid or tid with dose increments every 3 days. It can be combined with nortriptyline for enhanced effectiveness.
keyPoints: First-line treatment: Gabapentin
Initial dosing: 100-300 mg bid or tid
Dose increment: 50-100% every 3 days
Effective daily dose range: 900–3600 mg in divided doses
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · Treatment (part 2)

items.title: Use of Glucocorticoids in Palliative Care
items.content: Glucocorticoids like dexamethasone are effective for reducing inflammation-related pain and improving mood, energy, and appetite. They are particularly useful for bone pain and abdominal pain due to GI tract or liver distention.
keyPoints: Dexamethasone is preferred, given once a day.
Effective for bone pain and abdominal pain from GI tract or liver distention.
Main side effects include confusion, sleep difficulties, and fluid retention.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · Treatment (part 3)

items.title: Management of Constipation in Palliative Care
items.content: Constipation is common in palliative care patients due to opioids and anticholinergic effects. Proactive treatment with laxatives and stool softeners is essential.
keyPoints: Constipation frequency: 70–100% of palliative care patients.
Causes include opioids, tricyclic antidepressants, inactivity, and poor diet.
Preemptive use of a combination of laxative (e.g., senna) and stool softener (e.g., docusate).
Fiber is contraindicated with opioid use.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · Treatment (part 3)

items.title: Approach to Nausea in Palliative Care
items.content: Nausea management involves identifying the cause and targeting specific receptors. Metoclopramide or a serotonin type 3 receptor antagonist like ondansetron is recommended when no specific cause is identified.
keyPoints: Nausea frequency: up to 70% in advanced cancer patients.
Causes include metabolic changes, bowel obstruction, medications, and anxiety.
Treatment targets receptors at the GI tract, vestibular system, CTZ, and cerebral cortex.
Metoclopramide or ondansetron used when no specific cause is identified.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · Treatment (part 3)

items.title: Tailoring Antiemetic Therapy for Chemotherapy-Induced Nausea
items.content: When treating nausea induced by chemotherapy, it is crucial to tailor the antiemetic therapy based on the specific emetic risk posed by the chemotherapy drug. This approach involves using 5-HT3 receptor antagonists or neurokinin-1 antagonists like aprepitant for prevention rather than treatment after onset.
keyPoints: Prevention of postchemotherapy nausea is recommended over reactive treatment.
Tailor antiemetic strength to the specific emetic risk of the chemotherapy drug.
5-HT3 receptor antagonists or neurokinin-1 antagonists are preferred for prevention.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · Treatment (part 4)

items.title: Management of Dyspnea in End-of-Life Care
items.content: Dyspnea is a common and distressing symptom in end-of-life care, particularly among patients with lung cancer or metastases. Management focuses on symptomatic relief rather than addressing the underlying cause, which may be untreatable.
keyPoints: Over 50% to 75% of dying patients experience dyspnea near the end of life.
Oxygen supplementation is not consistently effective for dyspnea in end-stage cancer or cardiac failure.
Low-dose opioids can reduce central respiratory center sensitivity and alleviate dyspnea.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · Treatment (part 4)

items.title: Considerations for Using Cannabinoids in Palliative Care
items.content: The use of medical marijuana or oral cannabinoids for palliative treatment of nausea is controversial due to a lack of controlled trials. While there is some evidence supporting their efficacy, they are not as effective as 5-HT3 receptor antagonists and may cause cannabis hyperemesis syndrome.
keyPoints: No controlled trials conclusively show the effectiveness of cannabinoids for end-of-life nausea.
Cannabinoids can improve chemotherapy-induced nausea but are less effective than 5-HT3 receptor antagonists.
Older patients often tolerate cannabinoids poorly.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · Treatment (part 4)

items.title: Understanding and Managing Fatigue in Palliative Care
items.content: Fatigue is a prevalent symptom among terminally ill patients, affecting over 90% of them. It arises from various etiologies including disease processes, cytokines, secondary factors like anemia or dehydration, and psychological distress.
keyPoints: More than 90% of terminally ill patients experience fatigue/weakness.
Causes include underlying disease, cytokines (e.g., tumor necrosis factor), dehydration, anemia, infection, hypothyroidism, drug side effects.
Fatigue assessment relies on patient self-reporting; ECOG performance status is a practical clinical tool.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · Treatment (part 5)

items.title: Assessment Tools for Fatigue in Clinical Practice
items.content: While various scales exist to measure fatigue, the ECOG performance status and simple queries about daily activity are preferred in clinical settings due to their practicality.
keyPoints: ECOG performance status: 0 = normal activity; 1 = symptomatic without being bedridden; 2 = requiring some, but <50%, bed time; 3 = bedbound more than half the day; 4 = bedbound all the time.
Simple performance assessments are practical for clinical use and correlate with disease severity.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · Treatment (part 5)

items.title: Pharmacologic Interventions for Fatigue in End-of-Life Care
items.content: While few pharmacologic treatments target fatigue, glucocorticoids like dexamethasone and psychostimulants such as dextromamphetamine may be used to enhance energy levels. Their efficacy varies, with benefits often seen within the first month of treatment.
keyPoints: Glucocorticoids (e.g., Dexamethasone 8 mg/d) can increase energy and mood; benefit usually seen within the first month.
Psychostimulants (e.g., dextromamphetamine 5–10 mg PO, methylphenidate 2.5–5 mg PO) may enhance energy but lack strong evidence for cancer-related fatigue.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · Treatment (part 5)

items.title: Indications for Palliative Sedation
items.content: Palliative sedation is considered when patients experience severe, refractory symptoms such as pain or dyspnea that cannot be alleviated by conventional treatments. It is also indicated in cases of acute catastrophic symptoms like uncontrolled seizures.
keyPoints: Used for severe, refractory symptoms (e.g., pain, dyspnea) and acute catastrophic symptoms (e.g., uncontrolled seizures).
Considered as an intervention of last resort after other interventions have failed.
Potential abuse if used to hasten death or without patient's consent.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PALLIATIVE SEDATION

items.title: Methods and Medications for Palliative Sedation
items.content: Palliative sedation can be achieved by increasing opioid doses until unconsciousness, followed by continuous infusion. Midazolam is commonly used at 1–5 mg IV every 5–15 min, then a continuous infusion of 1 mg/h. Propofol at 5 µg/kg per min and other medications like levomepromazine, chlorpromazine, and phenobarbital are also options.
keyPoints: Opioid dose increase until unconsciousness followed by continuous infusion.
Midazolam: 1–5 mg IV every 5–15 min, then 1 mg/h continuous infusion.
Propofol: Continuous infusion of 5 µg/kg per min in hospital settings.
Other medications include levomepromazine, chlorpromazine, and phenobarbital.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PALLIATIVE SEDATION

items.title: Recognizing Major Depression vs. Normal Emotional Responses at End of Life
items.content: Depression in terminally ill patients can be distinguished from normal emotional responses by the persistence and intensity of symptoms. While sadness, anxiety, anger, and irritability are common and typically transient reactions to serious illness, persistent feelings of sadness and anxiety that lead to physical disability suggest major depression.
keyPoints: Up to 75% of terminally ill patients experience depressive symptoms.
~25% of terminally ill patients have major depression.
Persistent dysphoric mood, helplessness, hopelessness, and lack of interest are key indicators for diagnosing depression.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PSYCHOLOGICAL SYMPTOMS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.title: Screening Tools for Depression in Terminally Ill Patients
items.content: The PHQ-9 and PHQ-2 are recommended screening tools for diagnosing depression in terminally ill patients. These tools focus on assessing dysphoric mood, helplessness, hopelessness, and lack of interest over the past two weeks.
keyPoints: PHQ-9 and PHQ-2 are used to screen for depression near end-of-life.
Questions assess 'little interest or pleasure in doing things' and 'feeling down, depressed, or hopeless'.
Answer categories: not at all, several days, more than half the days, nearly every day.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PSYCHOLOGICAL SYMPTOMS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.title: Differential Diagnosis and Management of Depression in Terminal Illness
items.content: When diagnosing depression in terminally ill patients, it is crucial to exclude conditions that mimic depressive symptoms such as endocrinopathies (e.g., hypothyroidism, Cushing's syndrome), electrolyte abnormalities (e.g., hypercalcemia), and akathisia from dopamine-blocking antiemetics.
keyPoints: Exclude endocrinopathies like hypothyroidism and Cushing's syndrome.
Consider electrolyte abnormalities such as hypercalcemia.
Rule out akathisia, particularly from medications like metoclopramide and prochlorperazine.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PSYCHOLOGICAL SYMPTOMS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.title: Use of Psychostimulants in Terminally Ill Patients
items.content: Psychostimulants are preferred for terminally ill patients with poor prognosis, fatigue, or opioid-induced somnolence due to their rapid onset of action. They can be combined with traditional antidepressants and tapered after a few weeks.
keyPoints: Dextroamphetamine or methylphenidate: Start at 2.5–5.0 mg in the morning and noon, escalate up to 15 mg bid.
Modafinil: Start at 100 mg qd, increase to 200 mg if needed.
Pemoline: Start at 18.75 mg in the morning and noon; monitor liver function for prolonged use.
Side effects include initial anxiety, insomnia, and rarely paranoia.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PSYCHOLOGICAL SYMPTOMS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.title: SSRIs in Patients with Longer Prognosis
items.content: For patients with a prognosis of several months or longer, SSRIs are preferred due to efficacy and fewer side effects. Start at half the usual dose for healthy adults.
keyPoints: Fluoxetine starting dose: 10 mg once daily.
SSRIs can take up to 4 weeks to become effective.
Choice of SSRI based on past success, failure, and side effect profile.
For fatigue, use a more activating SSRI like fluoxetine; for anxiety/sleeplessness, use a sedating SSRI like paroxetine.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PSYCHOLOGICAL SYMPTOMS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.title: Potential of Psychedelic Drugs in Treating Depression
items.content: Psylocibin shows promise for treating depression, with rapid onset and lasting effects. It is not FDA approved but may be available in trials.
keyPoints: Single dose of 25 mg psylocibin effective in up to 70% of patients.
Rapid relief within 1 week that can last months.
Not FDA approved for general use; available through clinical trials.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PSYCHOLOGICAL SYMPTOMS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.title: Recognizing and Managing Terminal Delirium
items.content: Terminal delirium is common in the days and hours before death, affecting up to 85% of patients dying from cancer. It's characterized by altered cognition and consciousness, often with fluctuating levels and inattention.
keyPoints: Up to 85% of patients dying from cancer may experience terminal delirium.
Delirium is a global cerebral dysfunction with acute onset, fluctuating consciousness, and inattention.

Causes include metabolic encephalopathy, electrolyte imbalances, side effects of treatments (e.g., opioids, glucocorticoids), and multifactorial etiologies like dehydration exacerbating opioid-induced delirium.

Assessment involves recognizing new onset disorientation, impaired cognition, somnolence, fluctuating consciousness levels, or delusions with agitation; distinguishing from anxiety, depression, and dementia by altered consciousness.
Formal assessment tools may help distinguish delirium from other processes, but a reversible etiologic factor is found in fewer than half of all terminally ill patients.
Management includes discontinuing unnecessary medications, treating reversible causes (e.g., constipation, urinary retention), and supportive measures like providing a familiar environment.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PSYCHOLOGICAL SYMPTOMS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 3)

items.title: Distinguishing Delirium from Other Psychological Symptoms
items.content: Delirium must be distinguished from acute anxiety, depression, and dementia by its hallmark feature of altered consciousness, which is not typically present in the other conditions.
keyPoints: Altered consciousness distinguishes delirium from anxiety, depression, and dementia.
Hyperactive delirium involves confusion and agitation; hypoactive delirium includes sleep-wake reversal and decreased alertness.
Formal assessment tools like the Delirium Rating Scale can help distinguish delirium from dementia.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PSYCHOLOGICAL SYMPTOMS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 3)

items.title: Interventions for Terminal Delirium
items.content: Aggressive treatment of terminal delirium is crucial to allow patients lucidity for saying goodbye, reducing distress for family and caregivers, and mitigating bereavement difficulties.
keyPoints: Terminal care aims to provide lucidity for patients to say goodbye.
Delirium with agitation can cause distress to families and lead to bereavement difficulties.
Interventions include discontinuing unnecessary medications, treating reversible causes, and supportive measures like providing a familiar environment.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PSYCHOLOGICAL SYMPTOMS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 3)

items.title: First-line Pharmacologic Management of Delirium in Terminally Ill Patients
items.content: Haloperidol is the first-line therapy for managing delirium, typically administered at a low dose ranging from 1–3 mg/d every 6 hours. In some cases, doses up to 20 mg/d may be necessary. It can be given orally, subcutaneously, or intravenously, but not intramuscularly unless absolutely required.
keyPoints: Haloperidol is the first-line therapy for delirium in terminally ill patients.
Dosing typically starts at 1–3 mg/d every 6 hours, with some cases requiring up to 20 mg/d.
Can be administered PO, SC, or IV; IM injections are generally avoided.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PSYCHOLOGICAL SYMPTOMS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 4)

items.title: Use of Olanzapine for Delirium in Cancer Patients
items.content: Olanzapine, an atypical neuroleptic, is effective in resolving delirium in cancer patients and offers additional benefits such as antinausea, antianxiety, and weight gain. It is preferred for patients with longer life expectancies due to a lower risk of dysphoria and dystonic reactions, and can be used in those with hepatic or renal dysfunction.
keyPoints: Olanzapine effectively resolves delirium in cancer patients.
Dose: 2.5–5 mg PO bid; reaches steady state in a week.
Benefits include antinausea, antianxiety, and weight gain effects.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PSYCHOLOGICAL SYMPTOMS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 4)

items.title: Management of Insomnia in Patients with Advanced Illness
items.content: Insomnia is common among patients with advanced cancer and other end-stage conditions, occurring in 19–63% of those with advanced cancer. Management includes improving sleep hygiene, treating underlying anxiety or depression, and using medications like mirtazapine for sedation.
keyPoints: Insomnia occurs in 19–63% of patients with advanced cancer.
Management involves improving sleep hygiene and addressing underlying conditions.
Mirtazapine can be used for its sedative effects in depressed patients.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PSYCHOLOGICAL SYMPTOMS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 4)

items.title: Financial Toxicity at End-of-Life Care
items.content: End-of-life care can impose significant financial burdens on patients and families, leading to distress known as financial toxicity. In the U.S., a notable portion of families with end-stage cancer face major economic challenges.
keyPoints: A fifth to half of families report substantial financial burden from end-stage cancer care in the U.S.
Out-of-pocket costs for caregivers ranged from $4,645 to $10,547 (14-28% household income) within the last year of life.
10-30% of families sell assets or use savings/mortgages to cover healthcare costs.
40% of primary caregivers quit jobs due to caregiving demands.
Economic burdens are linked to adverse health outcomes and preferences for comfort care over life-prolonging treatments.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · SOCIAL NEEDS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.title: Importance of End-of-Life Conversations
items.content: End-of-life conversations between patients with advanced cancer and physicians can significantly reduce healthcare costs in the final week of life, improving quality of death.
keyPoints: Patients who had end-of-life discussions with physicians experienced lower healthcare costs in their last week.
Higher costs were associated with worse perceived quality of death.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · SOCIAL NEEDS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.title: Facilitating Family and Relationship Needs
items.content: Addressing the relational needs of terminally ill patients involves facilitating meaningful interactions with family and friends, which can ease the grief process for bereaved families.
keyPoints: Settling personal issues and saying goodbye are critical in end-of-life care.
Bereaved individuals who haven't said goodbye often face a more challenging grief process.
Facilitating family presence with unrestricted visiting hours can be beneficial.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · SOCIAL NEEDS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOCIAL NEEDS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 2) | EXISTENTIAL NEEDS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT
items.title: Impact of Caregiving on Mortality and Unmet Needs in Terminally Ill Patients
items.content: Caregivers, predominantly women, face substantial burdens when caring for terminally ill patients. This role is associated with higher mortality rates compared to non-caregivers. A significant portion of terminally ill patients experience unmet needs for nursing and personal care.
keyPoints: Three-quarters of family caregivers are women—wives, daughters, sisters, or daughters-in-law.
20% of terminally ill patients report substantial unmet needs for nursing and personal care.
Both bereaved and current caregivers have a higher mortality rate than non-caregiving controls.
Interventions should include inquiries about unmet needs and mobilization of community assistance.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · SOCIAL NEEDS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 2) | EXISTENTIAL NEEDS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT

items.subtopic: SOCIAL NEEDS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 2) | EXISTENTIAL NEEDS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT
items.title: Addressing Existential Needs in Terminally Ill Patients
items.content: Religion and spirituality often become more significant for terminally ill patients, with nearly 70% reporting increased religiosity or spirituality. However, about 20% feel less religious. Physicians should inquire about these aspects to identify specific needs and improve patient care.
keyPoints: Nearly 70% of terminally ill patients report becoming more religious or spiritual.
~20% become less religious, feeling cheated or betrayed by their illness.
Physicians should detect spiritual and existential needs through screening questions.
36% of respondents found clergy members comforting; physicians should inquire about the role of spirituality in a patient's life.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · SOCIAL NEEDS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT (part 2) | EXISTENTIAL NEEDS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT

items.subtopic: PALLIATIVE CARE SERVICES: HOW AND WHERE (part 1)
items.title: Understanding Hospice Care Enrollment and Duration
items.content: Hospice care in the United States is primarily funded by Medicare Part A, with specific eligibility criteria requiring two physicians to certify a prognosis of 6 months or less. The average length of hospice enrollment for Medicare beneficiaries was 92 days as of 2021, but the median stay was only 17 days. This discrepancy highlights that many patients are enrolled in hospice care very close to death.
keyPoints: Medicare Part A funds hospice services.
Two physicians must certify a prognosis of ≤6 months.
Average length of enrollment: 92 days; median stay: 17 days.
Over one-third of patients die within 7 days of hospice referral, and 1 in 10 dies within 72 hours.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PALLIATIVE CARE SERVICES: HOW AND WHERE (part 1)

items.subtopic: PALLIATIVE CARE SERVICES: HOW AND WHERE (part 1)
items.title: Financial Implications and Care Models for Hospice Services
items.content: Hospice care payments are per diem, covering a range of services including medical direction, nursing visits, and bereavement counseling. The Medicare Care Choices Model (MCCM) demonstrated cost savings by reducing ICU days, inpatient admissions, and emergency department visits, leading to a 14% reduction in expenditures per beneficiary.
keyPoints: Hospice payments are per diem, not fee-for-service.
Covered services include medical direction, nursing visits, and bereavement counseling.
MCCM reduced expenditures by 14%, with significant reductions in ICU days (38%), inpatient admissions (26%), and emergency department visits (14%).
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PALLIATIVE CARE SERVICES: HOW AND WHERE (part 1)

items.subtopic: PALLIATIVE CARE SERVICES: HOW AND WHERE (part 1)
items.title: Cognitive Biases Affecting Hospice Referrals
items.content: Physicians often refer patients to hospice care late due to cognitive biases, including systematic optimism in prognostication. Studies show that physicians overestimate survival by a factor of 5.3, with only one in five predictions within 33% of actual survival.
keyPoints: Cognitive biases lead to late hospice referrals.
Physicians overestimate patient survival by a factor of 5.3.
Only 20% of prognostications are within 33% of actual survival.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PALLIATIVE CARE SERVICES: HOW AND WHERE (part 1)

items.subtopic: PALLIATIVE CARE SERVICES: HOW AND WHERE (part 2)
items.title: Utilizing the 'Surprise Question' for Early Palliative Care Referral
items.content: The 'surprise question' is a clinical heuristic developed to predict patient outcomes and aid in timely referral to palliative care. Instead of predicting specific outcomes, it assesses clinician expectations about whether an event (e.g., death) would be surprising within a given timeframe.
keyPoints: Originally studied for 12-month mortality prediction in primary care.
Validated across various settings including dialysis and cancer patients.
Used to predict both overall 1-year and 1-month mortality.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PALLIATIVE CARE SERVICES: HOW AND WHERE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PALLIATIVE CARE SERVICES: HOW AND WHERE (part 2)
items.title: Benefits of Early Palliative Care Integration
items.content: Introducing palliative care earlier in a patient's illness can improve quality of life, reduce emergency room visits and hospitalizations, and facilitate smoother transitions to hospice care. This approach is supported by studies showing higher satisfaction and more home deaths among patients receiving early interdisciplinary palliative care.
keyPoints: Efforts are being made to provide palliative care before the last 6 months of life.
In-home palliative care leads to increased patient satisfaction and reduced emergency visits.
Palliative care consultations can be billed under Medicare Part B for nonhospice patients.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · PALLIATIVE CARE SERVICES: HOW AND WHERE (part 2)

items.title: Legal and Ethical Considerations in Withdrawing Life-Sustaining Treatment
items.content: Patients have a moral and legal right to refuse medical interventions, including life-sustaining treatments. This applies even if the patient is incompetent, provided there are clear indications of their preferences through advance care directives or proxies. However, courts may limit family decisions for conscious but incompetent patients who are not terminally ill.
keyPoints: Patients have a moral and legal right to refuse medical interventions.
Incompetent patients can have next of kin make decisions if no advance directive is present.
Courts restrict families from terminating life-sustaining treatments for conscious, incompetent, non-terminally ill patients.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · WITHDRAWING AND WITHHOLDING LIFE-SUSTAINING TREATMENT (part 1)

items.title: Criteria for Terminating Medical Interventions in Incompetent Patients
items.content: For incompetent patients without specific advance directives, decisions are guided by substituted judgment and best-interests criteria. Substituted judgment involves proxies imagining the patient's choices if competent, while best interests balance treatment benefits against burdens. Clinicians play a crucial role in explaining these to families.
keyPoints: Substituted-judgment criterion is often unreliable due to proxy inaccuracies.
Best-interests criterion evaluates treatments by balancing benefits and risks.
Physician involvement is essential in clarifying treatment benefits and burdens.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · WITHDRAWING AND WITHHOLDING LIFE-SUSTAINING TREATMENT (part 1)

items.title: Trends and Practices in Withholding Life-Sustaining Interventions
items.content: Withholding life-sustaining interventions is standard practice, with over 90% of patients dying without CPR. The frequency of CPR has significantly decreased from 49% in the late 1980s to just 1.8% by 2008. Practices vary widely among hospitals and ICUs, influenced more by physician preferences than standardized guidelines.
keyPoints: Over 90% of patients die without CPR.
CPR frequency decreased from 49% in 1987-1988 to 1.8% in 2001-2008.
Variation exists among hospitals and ICUs regarding intervention practices.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · WITHDRAWING AND WITHHOLDING LIFE-SUSTAINING TREATMENT (part 1)

items.title: Optimizing Comfort During Ventilator Withdrawal
items.content: To ensure patient comfort during ventilator withdrawal, it is essential to terminate neuromuscular blocking agents and administer sedatives and analgesics. This approach allows patients to express discomfort, aiding in the titration of medications and facilitating family interactions.
keyPoints: Terminate neuromuscular blocking agents before ventilator withdrawal.
Administer a bolus of midazolam (2–4 mg) or lorazepam (2–4 mg).
Follow with a morphine bolus (5–10 mg) and continuous infusion (50% of the bolus dose per hour).
Use IV scopolamine (100 µg/h) for significant upper airway secretions.
Adjust morphine doses based on respiratory distress or pain signs.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · WITHDRAWING AND WITHHOLDING LIFE-SUSTAINING TREATMENT (part 2)

items.title: Understanding Survival Post-Ventilator Withdrawal
items.content: The median time to death after ventilator withdrawal is approximately 1 hour, but up to 10% of patients may survive for more than a day. Women and older patients often have longer survival times post-extubation.
keyPoints: Median time to death after ventilator withdrawal: 1 hour.
Up to 10% of patients survive beyond 24 hours post-ventilator withdrawal.
Women and older patients tend to have longer survival durations.
Families should be informed about symptom management and uncertainty in survival duration.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · WITHDRAWING AND WITHHOLDING LIFE-SUSTAINING TREATMENT (part 2)

items.title: Understanding Futility in Medical Interventions
items.content: Futility in medical care refers to interventions that are unlikely to benefit the patient. It is categorized into physiologic, qualitative, and quantitative futility. Quantitative futility involves a treatment's ineffectiveness based on empirical data from past cases, but it often deviates from clinical practice due to subjective judgments.
keyPoints: Physiologic futility: intervention has no physiologic effect.
Qualitative futility: procedures that do not end total dependence on intensive care.
Quantitative futility: treatment deemed useless in the last 100 cases, but clinical predictions often differ.
More than one in five patients had a >10% chance of survival to hospital discharge despite being considered quantitatively futile by residents.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · FUTILE CARE (part 1)

items.title: Ethical and Legal Aspects of Futility
items.content: Physicians can ethically refuse treatments deemed futile, guided by professional societies' process-based approaches. Laws like the Futile Care Act provide a 'safe harbor' for physicians from liability when refusing life-sustaining interventions. These laws facilitate ethics consultations and potential unilateral withdrawal or transfer of care.
keyPoints: American Medical Association: respecting autonomy does not mandate specific interventions if deemed futile.
Futile Care Act (Texas, 1999) allows physicians to refuse futile treatments with legal protection.
19 state laws protect physician futility judgments; Texas law involves ethics consultation and potential unilateral withdrawal after 10 days of unresolved disagreement.
From 2007 data: 974 ethics committee consultations on futility cases; 65 rulings against families, treatment withdrawn for 27 patients.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · FUTILE CARE (part 1)

items.title: Distinguishing Between Euthanasia and Physician-Assisted Suicide
items.content: Euthanasia and physician-assisted suicide (PAS) are distinct from withdrawing life-sustaining care or symptom management. Terminating care is ethical, while PAS involves assisting in ending a patient's life, with varying definitions across countries.
keyPoints: Withdrawing life-sustaining care and managing symptoms like pain are considered ethical.
Euthanasia and PAS differ by country; e.g., Netherlands: PAD applies to terminating treatments, not providing life-ending pills.
In the UK, PAD involves prescribing life-ending interventions for terminally ill patients, while PAS is broader.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · FUTILE CARE (part 1)

items.title: Legal Status and Criteria for PAS/Euthanasia
items.content: PAS is legal in specific U.S. states, while euthanasia is not legalized anywhere in the U.S. Internationally, countries like the Netherlands and Canada have broader criteria beyond terminal illness, focusing on 'unbearable suffering'. In the U.S., strict conditions including a 15-day waiting period are required for PAS.
keyPoints: PAS legal in Washington, D.C., and 10 states: Oregon, Washington State, Montana, Vermont, California, Colorado, Hawaii, Maine, New Jersey, New Mexico
Euthanasia not legalized in any U.S. state
In the Netherlands and Canada, euthanasia/PAS criteria include 'unbearable suffering', not just terminal illness
U.S. PAS requires a terminal condition of <6 months and a 15-day waiting period
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · FUTILE CARE (part 2)

items.title: Prevalence and Demographics of Euthanasia/PAS Usage
items.content: Euthanasia and PAS are rare, with less than 10-20% of terminally ill patients considering them. In countries like the Netherlands and Belgium, these practices account for a small percentage of all deaths, while in Canada, usage increased by 30% after expanding eligibility criteria.
keyPoints: Fewer than 10–20% of terminally ill patients consider euthanasia/PAS
In the Netherlands (2022), 5.1% of all deaths were by euthanasia or PAS
In Belgium (2013), 2.5% of all deaths were by euthanasia
Canada saw a 30% increase in euthanasia/PAS use after expanding eligibility criteria
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · FUTILE CARE (part 2)

items.title: Motivations and Concerns for Euthanasia/PAS Requests
items.content: Pain is not the primary motivator for euthanasia or PAS; instead, loss of autonomy, inability to enjoy activities, and fear of losing dignity are more significant concerns. Depression and hopelessness are strongly associated with interest in these practices.
keyPoints: Loss of autonomy (86% OR, 83% WA), not being able to enjoy activities (89% OR, 83% WA), fear of losing dignity (62% OR, 69% WA) are top concerns
Depression and hopelessness are strongly linked with interest in euthanasia/PAS
Inadequate pain control is a less common motivator; only 31% in Oregon cite pain as the main reason for PAS
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · FUTILE CARE (part 2)

items.title: Motivations for Medically Assisted Suicide in Canada
items.content: In Canada, the primary reasons patients seek medically assisted suicide include loss of ability to engage in meaningful life activities and perform daily living tasks. A significant portion also cites inadequate pain or symptom control, and feeling like a burden.
keyPoints: Loss of ability to engage in meaningful life activities: 86%
Loss of ability to perform activities of daily living: 82%
Inadequate pain control: 59%
Inadequate symptom control: 47%
Feeling like a burden on family, friends, or caregivers: 35%
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · FUTILE CARE (part 3)

items.title: Complications and Realities of Euthanasia and PAS
items.content: Euthanasia and physician-assisted suicide (PAS) are not always painless or quick. Complications can occur, such as patients waking from a coma or regurgitating medications, with varying times to death.
keyPoints: 20% of euthanasia/PAS cases in the Netherlands had complications
Time between drug intake and coma: 1 min to 11 h
Time from drug intake to death: 1 min to 104 h
Median time from ingestion to coma: 5 min
Median time from ingestion to death: 25 min
Oregon (1998-2015): 53% had no complications, 44% had no data on complications, 2.4% had regurgitation as the only complication
Washington State (2014-2015): 1.4% had regurgitation, one patient had a seizure
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · FUTILE CARE (part 3)

items.title: Physician Experience and Response to Requests for Euthanasia or PAS
items.content: Many physicians encounter requests for euthanasia or PAS. In the U.S., a small percentage comply with these requests, while in the Netherlands, where legal, compliance is higher. Physicians should address underlying causes of such requests empathetically.
keyPoints: U.S.: 18% of physicians have received a request for PAS; 11% for euthanasia
3% complied with PAS requests; 5% with euthanasia requests in the U.S.
Netherlands: 77% of physicians have received requests; 60% performed interventions
Oregon (2022): 167 out of ~6300 physicians participated in PAS (2.6%)
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · FUTILE CARE (part 3)

items.subtopic: CARE DURING THE LAST HOURS (part 1) | CARE DURING THE LAST HOURS (part 2)
items.title: Understanding Terminal Changes and Family Reassurance
items.content: In the last hours of life, patients experience significant physical changes that can distress families. Educating caregivers about these changes helps reduce anxiety and ensures appropriate care.
keyPoints: Patients become bedbound with potential for pressure sores; turning must be balanced against discomfort.
Loss of gag reflex leads to 'death rattle'; scopolamine can manage secretions.
Respiratory changes include apnea or Cheyne-Stokes breathing; reassurance that these are not painful is crucial.
Informed families understand terminal changes (e.g., stopping eating) and reduce anxiety about interventions like feeding tubes.
Hearing and touch may be the last senses to cease, encouraging communication with the patient.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · CARE DURING THE LAST HOURS (part 1) | CARE DURING THE LAST HOURS (part 2)

items.subtopic: CARE DURING THE LAST HOURS (part 1) | CARE DURING THE LAST HOURS (part 2)
items.title: Planning for Death at Home
items.content: Proper planning and communication are essential when a patient is expected to die at home, ensuring the family knows how to handle the situation.
keyPoints: Inform families about signs of death: cessation of cardiac function/respiration, fixed pupils, body cooling.
Advise on contacting hospice or palliative care team instead of emergency services to avoid unwanted interventions.
Clarify that medical examiner need not be contacted unless required by state law.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · CARE DURING THE LAST HOURS (part 1) | CARE DURING THE LAST HOURS (part 2)

items.subtopic: CARE DURING THE LAST HOURS (part 1) | CARE DURING THE LAST HOURS (part 2)
items.title: Post-Death Family Support and Bereavement
items.content: After a patient's death, providing emotional support to the family is crucial. Actions like writing bereavement cards can help in comforting families.
keyPoints: Families may experience shock; allow time for emotional processing.
Bereavement cards or letters can communicate concern and honor the patient's memory.
Physician attendance at funerals, though not obligatory, offers support and closure.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · CARE DURING THE LAST HOURS (part 1) | CARE DURING THE LAST HOURS (part 2)

items.question: What is a central component of the interdisciplinary team approach in palliative care?
options: Pain and symptom management, spiritual and psychological care for the patient, and support for family caregivers
Exclusively medical treatment focusing on curing the illness
Providing only physical therapy to improve mobility
items.explanation: The interdisciplinary team approach in palliative care includes pain and symptom management, spiritual and psychological care for the patient, and support for family caregivers.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · HOSPICE AND THE PALLIATIVE CARE FRAMEWORK

items.subtopic: HOSPICE AND THE PALLIATIVE CARE FRAMEWORK
items.question: When is palliative care typically introduced according to recent changes in end-of-life care?
options: Only during the last few months of life
Many months before death, focusing on symptom relief
After hospice care has been initiated
items.explanation: Palliative care is introduced many months before death to focus on symptom relief and transition into hospice in the last few months.
items.reference: Palliative and End of Life Care · HOSPICE AND THE PALLIATIVE CARE FRAMEWORK

items.subtopic: HOSPICE AND THE PALLIATIVE CARE FRAMEWORK
items.question: What does phasing palliative care into end-of-life care allow patients to receive?
options: Palliative interventions only after being diagnosed as terminally ill
Interventions long before formal diagnosis of terminal illness or death within 6 months
Immediate hospice services upon any serious diagnosis
Financial planning services for end-of-life care
items.explanation: Phasing palliative care into end-of-life care allows patients to receive interventions long before they are formally diagnosed as terminally ill.

items.explanation: Invasive tests are best avoided as they may be uncomfortable and unlikely to change patient management.

items.question: Which U.S. Supreme Court ruling is mentioned regarding patients' rights in medical interventions?
options: Patients have a constitutional right to decide on refusing or terminating life-sustaining interventions.
Mentally incompetent patients cannot exercise any rights related to medical interventions.
Advance care directives are not constitutionally protected.
